reasoning: The timeframe from the knowledge cutoff (April 25, 2025) to the resolution date (July 1, 2025) is barely over two months. Revoking a 501(c)(3) status requires following strict IRS administrative procedures, which include audits, notices of proposed revocation, appeals, and eventual final determinations. This process typically takes years, not months. Even if the Trump administration attempts to bypass the standard IRS rules to revoke Harvard's status immediately, Harvard has the legal resources to file for an emergency injunction in federal court the very next day. Courts would almost certainly issue a stay or preliminary injunction, as a rushed revocation would likely violate the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) and due process protections, keeping Harvard's tax-exempt status legally intact while the issue is litigated. Any attempt to use legislation would take significant time, likely require 60 Senate votes to bypass a filibuster (Republicans only hold 53 seats), and face severe constitutional challenges such as being an unconstitutional bill of attainder. Due to the extremely tight timeline and the existence of administrative and judicial safeguards, it is highly implausible that Harvard will definitively lose its 501(c)(3) status before July 1, 2025. I am placing my probability at the minimum allowed bound of 0.05.
